title: practical english usage - Part 196
content:
We   can also describe situations like these by using when to introduce the shorter grammar •  252  whoever, whatever, etc Prisoners have to eat whatever they're given. (object of eat) Whatever you want is fine with me. (subject of is) I'll take whichever tent you're not using. (object of take) will get) Whichever climber gets to the top first will get a £5,000  prize. (subject of Send it to whoever pays the bills. (object of to) I'll marry whoever I like. (object of marry) Whoever told you that was lying. (subject of was lying) also a subject or object in the other clause.

A clause with whoever, whichever or whatever can be not only an adverbial, but 3 clauses      as  subjects      or  objects If you change your mind for whatever reason, just let me know.

Whatever problems you have, you can always come to me for help.

Whichever room you use, make sure you clean it up afterwards.

Whichever and whatever can also go with nouns as determiners.

Whatever you say, I don't think he's the right man. (object of say) Whoever you marry, make sure he can cook. (object of marry) Whoever directed this film, it's no good. (subject of directed) their clauses. (Note that whomever is not used in modern English.) Whoever, whichever and whatever can be the subjects or objects of the verbs in 2  whoever,         whichever           and   whatever         : subjects      and   objects Wherever you go, you'll find Coca-Cola.

However you travel, it'll take you at least three days.

Whichever of them you marry, you'll have problems.

With these conjunctions we  use present tenses when talking about the future.

You can come whenever you like.

I go to London I try to see Vicky.

People always want more, however rich they are.

However much  he eats, he never gets fat.

We're free all next week. You'll be welcome whichever day you come.

Whatever you do, I'll always love you. Keep calm, whatever happens.

I'm not opening the door, whoever you are.

Clauses         beginning                with         these          words         can  act as  adber    bials.

open' meanings.

and                have      similar person who '.  Whatever, whichever, however, whenever wherever Whoev          er    means          'it   doesn        ’ t   matter         who       ’ ,  'any    person             who       ’ , or     'the      unknown 1 meaning       and   use 252 whoever,      whatever,          etc Other         Adverbial               Clauses Section 23 grammar •  252  whoever, whatever, etc For other uses of whatever and however, see a good dictionary.   For who ever, what ever, etc, ►    627.

a formal alternative.

In American English, whatsoever is preferred in this sense; in British English it is I can see no point whatever in buying it.

Don't you have any regrets whatever?

After any and no, whatever can be used in British English to mean 'at all'.

8  whatever          meaning       'at  all' If you play football or tennis or whatever, it does take up a lot of time.

Would you like some orange juice or a beer or whatever?

Or whatever can mean 'or anything else’ .

'What would you like to do? We could go and see a film, or go swimming.’ sound rude.

Whatever is often used to mean 'I don’ t care' or 'I’ m  not interested'. This can ‘ Potatoes or rice? ‘ Whichever.’ (= I don’ t mind.) ‘ When shall we start? ‘ Whenever.’ (= Whenever you like.) In an informal style, these conjunctions are sometimes used as short answers.

7 informal       uses:   short    answers A grammar rule, however true (it is), is useless unless it can be understood.

After however + adjective, we  can leave out a pronoun + be.

A serious illness, whatever its nature, is almost always painful.

Whatever his problems, he has no right to behave like that.

the verb be, it is possible to leave out the verb.

In a clause like whatever his problems are, where whatever is the complement of 6 leaving      out   the   verb:   whatever          his    problems She's just written to me from Llandyfrdwy, wherever that may be.

He's written a book on the philosopher Matilda Vidmi, whoever she may be.
